channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Scientists May Have Detected The First Signature of a Black Hole's Event Horizon - ScienceAlert
item.description:

item.title: 'A weird result from an already weird hominin': Archaeologists discover all Homo naledi skeletons found in South African cave are female - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'A weird result from an already weird hominin': Archaeologists discover all Homo naledi skeletons found in South African cave are female  Live Science
  2. Fossils of archaic human Homo naledi found in a cave are the same sex. Scientists want to learn why  CNN

  4. A Controversial Ancient Human Burial Site Just Got More Mysterious  ScienceAlert

item.title: This Famously Pink Planet Is Hiding a Surprising Secret - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. This Famously Pink Planet Is Hiding a Surprising Secret  Gizmodo
  2. "Pink Planet" is surrounded by salty clouds, researchers using Webb telescope find  CBS News
  3. Webb telescope finds possible salt clouds on a pink alien world  Mashable
  4. For a decade the Pink Planet was too faint for any ground telescope to study — and when James Webb finally caught its spectrum, the best fit to the light was an atmosphere hazed with salt clouds  Space Daily
  5. Meet the exotic Pink Planet with salty clouds  EarthSky

item.title: A hidden fungal network longer than the distance to the Sun wraps the roots of 70% of Earth's plants, and the first global map finds it densest not under rainforests but wild grasslands - Earth.com
item.description:
1. A hidden fungal network longer than the distance to the Sun wraps the roots of 70% of Earth's plants, and the first global map finds it densest not under rainforests but wild grasslands  Earth.com

  3. A Hidden Universe: Underground Fungi Could Stretch to the Sun Nearly a Billion Times  atmos.earth
  4. Nearly All Plants Depend on Tiny Underground Fungi. The Microbes' Vast Global Networks Were Just Mapped for the First Time  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. Study: Fungal Networks So Massive They’d Span 10% of the Milky Way  Answers in Genesis

item.title: If you could drive a car straight up into the sky at highway speed, you would reach the edge of outer space in under an hour — the only thing protecting every living creature on Earth from the lethal vacuum is a layer of air thinner, relative to the planet, than the s - Space Daily

item.title: Voyager 1 has been travelling non-stop since 1977, faster than a speeding bullet every second of every day, and it has not yet reached the distance of a single light-year from Earth - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 has been travelling non-stop since 1977, faster than a speeding bullet every second of every day, and it has not yet reached the distance of a single light-year from Earth  Space Daily
  2. It's Official: Voyager 1 Will Break A Cosmic Record, Reaching 1 Light-Day From Earth On November 18, 2026  IFLScience
  3. The 49-Year-Old Spacecraft Still Talking From Deep Space  HackerNoon
  4. A probe is about to enter a realm humanity has only imagined until now  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  5. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until  19FortyFive

item.title: In 2004, a magnetar on the far side of the Milky Way unleashed a giant flare so intense it disturbed Earth’s ionosphere from tens of thousands of light-years away — and in just 0.2 seconds released as much energy as the Sun emits in roughly 250,000 years. - Space Daily
item.description: In 2004, a magnetar on the far side of the Milky Way unleashed a giant flare so intense it disturbed Earth’s ionosphere from tens of thousands of light-years away — and in just 0.2 seconds released as much energy as the Sun emits in roughly 250,000 years.  Space Daily

item.title: We tend to think of Neanderthals as a species that went extinct, but a Princeton geneticist found evidence of three waves of interbreeding over 250,000 years, leading him to argue Neanderthals didn't disappear — they were absorbed into us - Space Daily
item.description: We tend to think of Neanderthals as a species that went extinct, but a Princeton geneticist found evidence of three waves of interbreeding over 250,000 years, leading him to argue Neanderthals didn't disappear — they were absorbed into us  Space Daily

item.title: Astronauts aboard the International Space Station can see flashes of light even with their eyes closed — not from light entering the eye, but from high-energy space particles passing through their body and triggering the retina or visual pathway directly. - Space Daily
item.description: Astronauts aboard the International Space Station can see flashes of light even with their eyes closed — not from light entering the eye, but from high-energy space particles passing through their body and triggering the retina or visual pathway directly.  Space Daily

item.title: At its birth, Earth had no Moon. Then something perhaps the size of Mars slammed into the young planet, flinging molten debris into orbit that became the companion world now pulling our tides, steadying our seasons, and perhaps helping make Earth stable e - Space Daily

item.title: Europa’s surface is almost too clean. In a Solar System full of impactors, an icy moon should accumulate scars for billions of years — yet Europa has very few. The simplest explanation is also the strangest: the surface we see is young, constantly rewritten by fo - Space Daily
item.description: Europa’s surface is almost too clean. In a Solar System full of impactors, an icy moon should accumulate scars for billions of years — yet Europa has very few. The simplest explanation is also the strangest: the surface we see is young, constantly rewritten by fo  Space Daily

  4. "Once you show regeneration can be activated, it opens the door to new questions..." Mammals – including us – could regrow limbs say scientists  BBC Wildlife Magazine
  5. Scientists Discover Gene That One Day Could Help Humans Regrow Lost Limbs  ColombiaOne.com

item.title: Researchers at the University of California Riverside found in May 2026 that living systems distribute their amino acids more evenly than non-living chemistry does — a statistical pattern subtle enough that it went unnoticed for decades and powerful enough to - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Researchers at the University of California Riverside found in May 2026 that living systems distribute their amino acids more evenly than non-living chemistry does — a statistical pattern subtle enough that it went unnoticed for decades and powerful enough to  Space Daily
  2. Contact? Groundbreaking Israeli method may soon detect life in outer space  The Times of Israel

item.description: 6 Things Landscaping Pros Do to Keep Bugs and Animals From Burrowing in Their Yards Summer After Summer  The Spruce

item.title: Most of an octopus's brain cells live in its arms, not in its head — only about 8 percent of its 500 million neurons sit inside the central brain, with the rest spread across the eight arms and the optic lobes behind its eyes, in a body design some neuroscientists des - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Most of an octopus's brain cells live in its arms, not in its head — only about 8 percent of its 500 million neurons sit inside the central brain, with the rest spread across the eight arms and the optic lobes behind its eyes, in a body design some neuroscientists des  Space Daily
  2. This shell-less ocean creature evolved 140 million years ago: Scientists say it is the closest thing to an alien on Earth  The Times of India
  3. They have nine brains, three hearts and they dream: interacting with an octopus is the closest thing to meeting an alien  yourweather.co.uk
